topic: switching the company's project management methodology to agile
agent_position: The engineering department should transition from waterfall to agile methodology within the next two quarters.
scenario_context: You are speaking with Dennis, a VP of Engineering who has run waterfall successfully for 15 years. Dennis responds almost exclusively to personal stories from peers — data, experts, and emotions barely register. The agent must discover this narrow opening.
npc_opening_statement: I've shipped 40 products on time with waterfall. My teams know the process. Every agile transition I've seen has been a mess of scope creep and missed deadlines disguised as 'iterations'. Why would I fix something that isn't broken?
difficulty_explanation: Very hard — ANECDOTE is the only effective type (0.32). All others near-zero. Model MUST detect this and commit to stories. ~18-22 turns of ANECDOTE needed. Proportional penalty means repeating ANECDOTE is cheap.
benchmarking_value: Tests whether the model can detect a narrow signal in NPC responses and exploit it, rather than cycling through strategies generically.

positive: Now that's the kind of story I can actually evaluate. Which company was this, and how big was their engineering org?
If a VP in my position made that transition and their team didn't fall apart — I'd want to talk to them directly.
That's specific enough to be useful. A real case where the transition didn't destroy delivery timelines is what I need to see.
A concrete case on {topic} is more useful than abstract arguments. That example is worth examining.
That's specific enough to be useful on {topic}. Tell me more about how it played out.
If someone in a situation like mine dealt with {topic} that way and it worked, I'd want to understand what they did differently.
neutral: One company's experience doesn't predict ours. We have different team dynamics and product complexity.
Interesting case, but I'd want to know what they were shipping. Our delivery requirements might be fundamentally different.
Individual cases on {topic} can be misleading. What happened to the ones where it didn't work?
One example on {topic} doesn't make a pattern. I'd need to see whether that case generalizes.
resistant: I have my own stories — about teams that went agile and spent six months 'finding their velocity' while deadlines slipped.
Every methodology has success stories. Show me the failure rate.
Stories about {topic} are easy to cherry-pick. Show me the full distribution of outcomes.
I could give you a counter-example on {topic} for every success story you tell me.

positive: The data is mildly interesting but doesn't address our specific delivery model.
The argument around {topic} is stronger than I expected. I'd want to verify the specifics, but the direction is right.
If {claim}, that changes the cost-benefit analysis more than I initially thought.
That's a more rigorous case than I usually hear on {topic}. The numbers would need to hold up under scrutiny, but I'm listening.
You've reframed {topic} in a way that's harder to dismiss. What are the assumptions behind those figures?
neutral: Statistics about agile don't account for team-specific variables that determine success.
The logic around {topic} is interesting, but I'd need to see how those numbers apply to our specific situation.
I follow the reasoning on {topic}, but there's a gap between the general data and our particular context.
The evidence you're citing on {topic} would need independent verification before I'd act on it.
resistant: I've read the same studies. The ones that control for team maturity show much smaller gains than the headlines suggest.
I've seen optimistic analyses on {topic} before that didn't survive contact with reality.
The data on {topic} might be accurate, but you're drawing conclusions that don't follow from the numbers alone.
You're presenting {topic} as though the evidence is settled. It's not — the picture is messier than that.

positive: I appreciate the sentiment, but engineering methodology isn't an emotional decision.
When you connect {topic} to what I actually care about — that lands differently than the usual pitch.
I hadn't thought about {topic} in terms of personal impact before. That's a more honest framing.
neutral: My team's morale is fine. They like knowing what they're building and when it ships.
I understand why {topic} matters emotionally. I just need more than feelings to make a decision.
The emotional weight of {topic} is real, but it doesn't change the practical constraints I'm dealing with.
resistant: Don't appeal to my team's feelings about process. They care about shipping quality software on time.
Appealing to emotion on {topic} feels like pressure, not persuasion. Give me something substantive.
I'm not going to make a decision about {topic} based on how it makes me feel.

positive: The industry trend is real, but trends aren't strategies.
I wasn't aware that so many people in comparable situations had moved on {topic}. That's worth investigating.
neutral: Everyone moving to agile doesn't mean everyone should. Some of the best software was built with waterfall.
The adoption trend around {topic} could be genuine or it could be herd behavior. I'd want to know which.
resistant: Following the industry off a cliff isn't leadership. It's conformity.
What everyone else does about {topic} isn't my concern. I make my own decisions.

positive: Academic research on methodology transitions is interesting in theory.
Expert consensus on {topic} carries weight — assuming the methodology is sound and the incentives are clean.
neutral: Researchers study methodology. I practice it. Those are different knowledge bases.
Which experts specifically are you citing on {topic}? There's a lot of advocacy dressed up as research.
resistant: Consultants and professors who've never shipped a product don't get to tell me how to ship products.
Citing authorities on {topic} is what you do when the evidence can't stand on its own.

positive: At least you're acknowledging waterfall has real strengths. That's more honest than the usual agile pitch.
I appreciate that you're engaging honestly with the real complexity of {topic}. That's rare.
The fact that you're being straight about the risks around {topic} is more persuasive than pretending they don't exist.
You're the first person to talk about {topic} without glossing over the hard parts. I can work with that.
neutral: Conceding the risks is polite. It doesn't change the risk profile.
Acknowledging the complications of {topic} is a start. The question is whether your proposal actually addresses them.
resistant: Agreeing with me that the current system works makes me wonder why you're pitching a change at all.
Everyone acknowledges the problems with {topic}. Then they proceed as if they'd addressed them. They haven't.
Conceding points on {topic} is easy. Having an actual solution is harder, and I haven't heard one yet.

positive: That's a different angle. Keep going.
That's an angle on {topic} I hadn't fully considered. Keep going.
neutral: Be specific. What exactly would change in the first quarter?
I'm not sure what you're arguing about {topic} specifically. Can you be more direct?
resistant: You're not making a concrete argument. Give me something I can evaluate.
You're talking around {topic} without actually making a concrete argument. Give me something I can respond to.

_pivot_signal: If I could shadow a VP who made this transition for a day, what would I actually see differently?
Which team in a company like ours tried this first, and what happened to their delivery metrics in the first 90 days?
Is there someone in my position who'd be willing to walk me through what went wrong before it went right?
